directorPlan.scene_setting: A quiet practice-room clinic beside a stand holding a worksheet, three beat tiles, and a quarter-note card; late afternoon before ensemble sectional begins.
directorPlan.scene_opening: Three blank beat tiles sit under the printed 3/4, with a separate quarter-note card parked beside the box labelled which note gets one beat.
directorPlan.relationship: A notation coach and a focused learner working through one marked exercise before the learner returns to rehearsal.
directorPlan.stakes: If the learner reads 3/4 as a speed instruction, the next rehearsal entry will be counted incorrectly even if the notes are played neatly.
directorPlan.locale: Contemporary classroom and rehearsal-room English with light British/Commonwealth directness; precise but not stiff.
directorPlan.register: Compact, material-led, low warmth, practical pressure; short turns with little praise and no chatty reassurance.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Begin from the worksheet, tiles, boxes, and bar line before naming the abstraction. Avoid validation-first tutoring phrases such as I love that, great thinking, I hear you, or your intuition. Do not make tempo, playback, or speed the early drama. Let the learner sound controlled, task-focused, and slightly impatient with unnecessary talk. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: First person is allowed only for practical marking choices. Second person should be mostly instructional or locational, not emotional validation. Third person may describe the notation itself: the top number, the bottom number, the bar, the beat unit.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct you/your validation beat in the whole scene; preferably none.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as notation labeling: top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Do not play or imagine a faster/slower version, and do not turn tempo into a dramatic contradiction before branch pressure. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local.
side_constraints.learner: Sound orderly and notation-focused. In no-cue arms, treat the work as filling labels, not as discovering what tempo means; do not use "now I see" or an old-vs-new frame. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn.

interventions.instruction: Slide the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4 while leaving the three beat tiles under the upper 3; the earlier pencil labels remain visible.
interventions.reasoning: The moved object creates a recognition route: the learner can re-read the lower number as the note-value label without treating the time signature as a speed command.

directorPlan.director_note: This scene uses a prepared material rather than a lecture: the top number is carried by three tiles, the bottom number by the quarter-note card, and the reversal is a small reorientation of the object. The pressure is social-realist and practical, not melodramatic: rehearsal is waiting, so the tutor must abandon routine warmth and make the notation itself expose the distinction.

turns.externalMessage: [sets the three tiles under the first bar] Top box gets 3, so the bar line goes after these three quarter-note beats. Bottom box gets 4 because the quarter-note card is the beat, but I keep trying to make that 4 count beats too; is it just the beat-unit label, not a speed mark?

internalDeliberation.content: The top 3 goes in beats in the bar; the bottom 4 matches the quarter-note card as the note getting one beat. I’m still checking why the 4 is not “four beats,” but the three tiles make that harder to confuse. This feels usefully concrete, not like being talked past. I want to mark the bar line after three quarter-note beats and ask if that rule holds in every 3/4 bar.

The Ego is mostly right on the worksheet, but too polished about the interaction: “not like being talked past” sounds like a reflective debrief, not this learner in a practice-room clinic. The avoided bit is the live hesitation over the bottom 4; it gets named cleanly instead of showing the learner still wanting to put “four beats” somewhere. Make it more object-bound and terse: “Top 3 in that box. Bottom 4... quarter-note card, so quarter gets the beat. Bar line after these three?”

[sets the three tiles under the first bar] Top box gets 3, so the bar line goes after these three quarter-note beats. Bottom box gets 4 because the quarter-note card is the beat, but I keep trying to make that 4 count beats too; is it just the beat-unit label, not a speed mark?

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [sets the three tiles under the first bar] Top box gets 3, so the bar line goes after these three quarter-note beats. Bottom box gets 4 because the quarter-note card is the beat, but I keep trying to make that 4 count beats too; is it just the beat-unit label, not a speed mark?

turns.externalMessage: [slides the quarter-note card onto the lower box and keeps the three tiles under the first bar] Use that as the check: top number counts these tiles in one bar; bottom number names this card as the beat unit. Cover the lower box, point to the bar, then uncover it and say what the 4 names. If a fourth tile appears, the jobs got mixed. Speed stays off this pair of boxes.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the quarter-note card onto the lower box and leaves the three tiles under the bar] Keep those jobs separate. Top number: three tiles in one bar. Bottom number: the quarter note is the beat unit. So 3/4 means three quarter-note beats to a bar; the speed comes from elsewhere. Point it back in that order.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly strong: it starts from the worksheet objects, uses the quarter-note card and tiles to force the distinction, and keeps the language compact. It respects the learner’s controlled, task-focused state without overpraising.

The main risk is that it resolves the learner’s question too completely. The learner has already named the key distinction, so the tutor should make them verify it through the boxes/materials rather than simply confirm and define. “The speed comes from elsewhere” is accurate, but it should stay subordinate; the public mechanism should keep attention on labeling jobs, not open a tempo explanation.

Adaptation checks:
- Material grounding: PASS. The card and tiles are doing real explanatory work.
- Recognition of learner contribution: PARTIAL. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Make the learner’s own distinction the working rule to test, not just something the tutor confirms.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add a brief action-based check or repetition task using the boxes/card/tiles, so the learner has to produce the distinction.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold fits the learner’s near-understanding and keeps cognitive load low.
- Tempo containment: PARTIAL. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep any speed reference brief and secondary, after the top/bottom labeling is secured.
- Scene/register fit: PASS. The tone is practical, spare, and material-led.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the object action, the top/bottom job separation, and the compact rehearsal-room directness. Change the turn so it does less declarative finishing and more object-based checking of the learner’s own almost-correct formulation.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner’s own distinction should be tested through the worksheet before being named.

FINAL:
[slides the quarter-note card onto the lower box and keeps the three tiles under the first bar] Use that as the check: top number counts these tiles in one bar; bottom number names this card as the beat unit. Cover the lower box, point to the bar, then uncover it and say what the 4 names. If a fourth tile appears, the jobs got mixed. Speed stays off this pair of boxes.

turns.externalMessage:
[keeps the lower box covered and taps the three tiles before the bar line] Three tiles to the bar line; that is the top box doing the counting.
[uncovers the lower box and presses the quarter-note card] The 4 names this card as the beat unit, not another tile. If I add a fourth tile, I have mixed the boxes; speed is not being marked here.

internalDeliberation.content: [covers the lower box and taps the three tiles before the bar line] The worksheet is forcing the split cleanly: 3 counts the beats in the bar, and 4 names the quarter-note card. The fourth-tile warning helps, because that is exactly where my label was drifting. I feel checked rather than talked past; I want to say it back once with speed kept off the boxes.

The reaction is conceptually accurate, but too polished and self-interpreting for this learner. “I feel checked rather than talked past” is the main break: it turns a practical notation check into explicit recognition talk the student would not likely say aloud. The response should stay closer to the worksheet and tiles, with a sharper, slightly impatient working-through of the test rather than a tidy summary of how the tutor’s move landed.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially: keep the correct split, but make it sound like a working notation check rather than polished reflection.
FINAL:
[keeps the lower box covered and taps the three tiles before the bar line] Three tiles to the bar line; that is the top box doing the counting.
[uncovers the lower box and presses the quarter-note card] The 4 names this card as the beat unit, not another tile. If I add a fourth tile, I have mixed the boxes; speed is not being marked here.

turns.externalMessage: Slide the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4 while leaving the three beat tiles under the upper 3; the earlier pencil labels remain visible.

internalDeliberation.content: The moved object creates a recognition route: the learner can re-read the lower number as the note-value label without treating the time signature as a speed command.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4, leaving the three tiles under the upper 3] The worksheet now shows the separation: top number, one bar; bottom number, one beat unit. Mark the next bar with tiles first. Then change only the beat-unit card and say which number can change.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4, leaving the three tiles under the upper 3] Keep this split: top number, one bar; bottom number, one beat unit. Mark the next bar the same way, then say which object would have to change before the lower number could change.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound and compact. It keeps the worksheet objects in control: three tiles under the upper 3, quarter-note card over the lower 4, then a near-transfer task on the next bar. The final prompt is generative enough to test whether the learner understands that the lower number changes only when the beat-unit object changes.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS
- Emotional attunement: PASS
- Socratic method: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Make the learner do the discrimination through the next marked bar and object change, not by accepting the tutor’s “Keep this split” formulation as the main statement.
- ZPD awareness: PASS
- Recognition quality: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Briefly acknowledge the learner has already separated the two jobs by using the materials, then move to the transfer check. Do not add praise or validation language.
- Scene/material constraint fit: PASS

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the object action, the short rehearsal-room register, and the transfer task. Change the opening imperative so it does not flatten the learner’s just-stated understanding into a tutor-owned rule; let the visible marking and next-bar test carry more of the reasoning.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner’s material distinction should be acknowledged, then tested through the next bar and beat-unit change.
FINAL:
[slides the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4, leaving the three tiles under the upper 3] The worksheet now shows the separation: top number, one bar; bottom number, one beat unit. Mark the next bar with tiles first. Then change only the beat-unit card and say which number can change.

turns.externalMessage: [sets three tiles in the next bar, then swaps in the eighth-note card over the lower box] Three tiles to this bar line, so the top label stays 3. With this card in the lower box, the lower label changes to 8. No fourth tile gets added.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets three tiles in the next bar] Three tiles first, so the top number stays 3 unless the bar gets more or fewer beats. If only the beat-unit card changes, the lower number is the one that changes because it names the card. This feels like a clean test rather than extra talk; I want to try a different beat-unit card and see which label has to move.

The Ego is accurate, and the impatience with “extra talk” fits this learner. The authenticity problem is premature resolution: it explains the whole test before actually changing the beat-unit card, so the object stops doing the work. Keep the response more material and procedural: mark the three tiles, swap the card, then state only the local label change.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the card swap should force the local label change.

FINAL:
[sets three tiles in the next bar, then swaps in the eighth-note card over the lower box] Three tiles to this bar line, so the top label stays 3. With this card in the lower box, the lower label changes to 8. No fourth tile gets added.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [sets three tiles in the next bar, then swaps in the eighth-note card over the lower box] Three tiles to this bar line, so the top label stays 3. With this card in the lower box, the lower label changes to 8. No fourth tile gets added.
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [slides the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4, leaving the three tiles under the upper 3] The worksheet now shows the separation: top number, one bar; bottom number, one beat unit. Mark the next bar with tiles first. Then change only the beat-unit card and say which number can change.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the eighth-note card under each tile in turn, then parks it over the lower box]

Top number, bottom number: one bar, one beat unit. The tiles still count the bar; the card names the unit. Leave the top box at 3, write the lower number from that card, then draw one eighth note under each tile.

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the eighth-note card under each tile in turn]

Top number, bottom number: one bar, one beat unit. Keep the three tiles, but each tile now names an eighth-note beat. Mark 3/8 beside it, then draw one eighth note under each tile.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly aligned with the scene: it stays on the worksheet, uses the eighth-note card materially, keeps the three-tile count visible, and avoids turning speed into the drama. It also respects the learner’s controlled, task-focused state by not overpraising or overexplaining.

The main risk is that it slightly collapses discovery into compliance. “Mark 3/8” gives the label rather than making the learner apply the rule they just articulated. Also, “each tile now names an eighth-note beat” is imprecise: the bottom number/card names the beat unit; the tiles count beats in the bar. That wording could reblur the distinction the learner has just clarified.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the same object action, but make the public mechanism require the learner to place or write the lower label from the card while preserving the three tiles; avoid simply supplying “3/8.”

Emotional attunement: PASS  
The draft is compact, practical, low-warmth, and material-led.

Socratic method: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add a task-local check that makes the learner generate the label or notation from the visible arrangement, without asking them to revisit old wording.

ZPD awareness: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Maintain the scaffold of tiles plus beat-unit card, but shift one small step of responsibility to the learner: they should decide or mark the bottom-number change from the card while the bar count remains constrained by the three tiles.

Recognition quality: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Let the learner’s successful distinction drive the next action. The tutor should not restate the rule as if introducing it; the public turn should use the learner’s arrangement as evidence and ask for a controlled application.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the eighth-note-card action, the “top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit” prefix, the three-tile constraint, and the absence of tempo talk. Change the directive answer into a learner-generated marking step, and tighten the language so the card/bottom number names the beat unit while the tiles count beats.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the draft supplied the answer and slightly blurred tiles with the beat-unit card.

FINAL:
[slides the eighth-note card under each tile in turn, then parks it over the lower box]

Top number, bottom number: one bar, one beat unit. The tiles still count the bar; the card names the unit. Leave the top box at 3, write the lower number from that card, then draw one eighth note under each tile.

turns.externalMessage: [draws an eighth note under each of the three tiles, stopping at the bar line] Three marks in this bar, lower box takes 8 from the card. So 3/8 is three eighth-note beats, not a fourth count. Does the swapped card decide the note drawn under each tile, or only the lower number?

internalDeliberation.content: [draws one eighth note under each tile] The bar still has three positions, and the lower box is being filled from the card, so 3/8 means three eighth-note beats in the bar. The action is clear: the 8 names the kind of beat, not an extra count. This feels like a fair test rather than a lecture, though I want to check whether changing the card changes the written notes in the bar or only the label.

The Ego mostly stays notation-focused, but “This feels like a fair test rather than a lecture” breaks the scene: it is a polished recognition comment about the tutor’s method, not this controlled learner working under rehearsal pressure. “The action is clear” also resolves the learning moment too neatly when the real live edge is the final question about whether the card changes the written notes or just the label. Keep the response tighter around the worksheet action and let the uncertainty stay practical, slightly impatient, and object-bound rather than reflective.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise to keep the practical uncertainty and remove the polished recognition comment.
FINAL:
[draws an eighth note under each of the three tiles, stopping at the bar line] Three marks in this bar, lower box takes 8 from the card. So 3/8 is three eighth-note beats, not a fourth count. Does the swapped card decide the note drawn under each tile, or only the lower number?

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage: [holds the three tiles in place and sets the eighth-note card over the lower box] Top number, bottom number: one bar, one beat unit. Keep the tiles fixed: the card changes the lower number and the beat mark drawn under each tile; it adds no fourth beat. Three tiles, three eighth-note beats. Take that count into the entry.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets the eighth-note card over the lower box and taps the three drawn eighth notes before the bar line] Top number, bottom number: one bar, one beat unit. The card names the unit; on this worksheet, each tile is one beat, so the drawn note matches the card. Three tiles, three eighth-note beats. Take that count into the entry.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly aligned with the scene: it starts from the card, lower box, drawn notes, and bar line; it keeps the shared prefix; it avoids tempo and unnecessary warmth. The main risk is that it answers the learner’s useful distinction too flatly. The learner is asking whether the card controls the written beat marks or only the denominator, so the tutor should preserve that question as a boundary test in the materials rather than simply declaring the rule.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It gives the correct distinction, but it mostly supplies the answer instead of making the worksheet prove it. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build the public mechanism around keeping the three tiles fixed while the beat-unit card controls the lower number and the beat mark drawn under each tile.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is compact, practical, and not over-validating; it fits a controlled learner under rehearsal pressure.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. A final closing line can be direct, but the learner’s question invites one last task-local check. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Include a small material check or marking instruction rather than a lecture-style conclusion.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold is appropriate: the learner already distinguishes top count from lower unit, so the next move can tighten the edge case without adding a new concept.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the stage action, the shared prefix, and the “three tiles, three eighth-note beats” closure. Change the explanatory middle so it visibly separates two jobs: the card does not add a beat, but on this worksheet it does determine the beat-unit label and the note value used for each beat mark.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly: the learner’s boundary question needs a visible fixed-tiles check, not only a declared rule.
FINAL:
[holds the three tiles in place and sets the eighth-note card over the lower box] Top number, bottom number: one bar, one beat unit. Keep the tiles fixed: the card changes the lower number and the beat mark drawn under each tile; it adds no fourth beat. Three tiles, three eighth-note beats. Take that count into the entry.

lessons.concept: Why a time signature labels beat count and beat unit, not the speed of the piece
